summary: DHH observes a small protest passing by his Copenhagen window and reflects on the nature of demonstrations. While a larger protest filled the city center, this modest group made an outsized impression through sheer noise and energy. He questions whether protests serve the demonstrators themselves more than their intended targets, concluding that this is probably often the case.
key_points: A small group can create a disproportionately large impression through volume and energy
The spectacle of protest — drums, chanting, honking — can exceed the substance of its numbers
Demonstrations may function more as catharsis for participants than as persuasion of opponents
money_quotes: We don't need to be many, just loud!
I wonder how often demonstrations mean more to the people demonstrating than to those demonstrated against?
key_insight: The value of a demonstration often lies more in what it does for the demonstrators than in what it achieves against the demonstrated.
